Were You Asked This?
Design CoderPad

Design a collaborative coding platform similar to CoderPad that allows users to write, edit, and execute code in real-time with features like syntax highlighting, multiple language support, and live collaboration for technical interviews.

Asked at:

OpenAI

Meta

LinkedIn

CoderPad is a collaborative, real-time coding and execution environment used in technical interviews where multiple participants can write, edit, and run code together with syntax highlighting and rich language tooling. Think of it as Google Docs meets a coding competition platform, with a built-in, sandboxed code runner that streams output live while you type and execute.

Interviewers ask this because it blends two hard problems: low-latency real-time collaboration (presence, conflict resolution, cursor syncing) and safe, scalable code execution across many languages (resource isolation, queuing, streaming logs). A strong answer shows you can decompose the system, pick the right collaboration model (OT/CRDT), isolate untrusted code, and meet tight latency and reliability expectations—skills that map to many production systems beyond interviews.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to create or join a coding session via a shareable link with clear roles (interviewer/candidate) and permissions.

Users should be able to edit code together in real time with syntax highlighting, cursor presence, and basic IDE features (indentation, autocomplete-lite).

Users should be able to execute code in multiple languages with optional stdin, and see live stdout/stderr and exit status streamed back.

Users should be able to save, restore, and replay session history (code edits and runs) for review or feedback.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you achieve low-latency, globally distributed real-time collaboration (e.g., <150 ms echo) as sessions span regions?

How will you handle concurrent edits and conflict resolution (OT vs CRDT), especially under high contention and intermittent connectivity?

How will you design secure, scalable, and cost-efficient sandboxed code execution across many languages?

How will you stream stdout/stderr and interactive I/O reliably to many clients without overwhelming the system?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Collaborative editing, presence indicators, and live stdout/stderr require low-latency bidirectional updates with efficient fanout. The real-time updates pattern (e.g., WebSockets with room-based broadcasting) is central to making the editor feel instantaneous and reliable.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Multiple users frequently edit the same document, creating high contention on shared state. Applying contention strategies (OT/CRDT, server-authoritative ordering, sharding by room) prevents lost updates, cursor jumps, and divergent document states.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

Code execution is an external, potentially long-running job that must be queued, sandboxed, and monitored. The long-running tasks pattern helps you design runners, timeouts, retries, cancellation, and result streaming without blocking interactive paths.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis excels at ephemeral, low-latency coordination: pub/sub for room fanout, Redis Streams for ordered event buffers (stdout/stderr), presence tracking with TTLs, distributed locks for leader election in a shard, and rate limiting to protect hot rooms.

Read More
PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL provides durable storage for pads, users, ACLs, saved snapshots, and session histories with strong consistency, transactions, and indexing. It’s a solid default for metadata and versioned snapshots while hot edits live in memory.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka is useful when you need durable, ordered streams for execution events, audit logs, or cross-region replication of room deltas at scale. It decouples producers (runners, region gateways) from consumers (recording service, analytics, replay) with backpressure.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Google Docs

Both require real-time collaborative editing with conflict resolution (OT/CRDT), presence, and low-latency broadcasting. The architectural approach to rooms, sharding, and convergence closely parallels a live document editor.

Read
Practice
LeetCode

Both execute untrusted code across many languages with sandboxing, resource limits, job queues, and streaming output. The runner fleet, image/version management, and handling of stdin/stdout are shared concerns.

Read
Practice
Job Scheduler

Both involve dispatching, monitoring, and controlling many concurrent jobs with retries, timeouts, and quotas. Designing a robust execution pipeline and worker pool maps directly to scheduling code runs safely and efficiently.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

No clear concurrency/conflict-resolution model for collaborative edits

Hand-waving about ‘last write wins’ will cause lost updates and cursor chaos under contention. Without OT/CRDT or a server-authoritative ordering scheme, documents will diverge and users will see inconsistent states.

Insecure or weakly isolated code execution environment

Running arbitrary code without strong isolation (containers/microVMs, cgroups, seccomp, network egress controls) invites escapes, abuse, and resource exhaustion. This is a showstopper for trust, compliance, and stability.

Centralized bottleneck or sticky sessions for rooms

A single stateful server handling all room traffic or mandatory sticky sessions limit elasticity and hurt failover. Losing that node drops entire sessions and prevents horizontal scaling needed for interview-day spikes.
